content.text: What Is Testing Agents in CI/CD?

Continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines automate the path from a code change to a running production system. For traditional software, that path involves compiling, running deterministic unit and integration tests, and gating on a green result. Agentic AI systems introduce a twist: the core behaviour of an agent is produced by a language model, which is non-deterministic, expensive to call, and impossible to fully specify with an input–output table. Testing agents in CI/CD therefore requires a layered strategy that combines classical software-testing discipline with evaluation frameworks designed specifically for probabilistic systems.

The goal is to give every pull request a fast, cheap, high-signal verdict on whether the agent still behaves correctly, whether its quality metrics are above a baseline, and whether its infrastructure dependencies are wired up properly — all without requiring a human to manually probe the system after every commit. A well-designed agent test suite covers at least three layers:
that mock the LLM and tool calls,
that run the full agent loop against stubbed external services, and
that invoke a real (or a cheaper judge) model against a fixed dataset to measure output quality.

The discipline also encompasses how CI artifacts (traces, scores, diffs) are stored and surfaced in pull requests so that regressions are self-explanatory, and how the pipeline gates staging and production deployments so that a drop in agent quality blocks the release automatically.

Deploying a new version of an agent without automated quality checks is qualitatively riskier than deploying traditional software. A bug in a conventional service typically surfaces as a crash or a wrong numerical result — symptoms that are easy to detect with assertions. An agent regression surfaces as subtly worse answers: slightly less accurate retrieval, occasional hallucinations, increased refusal rate on valid inputs, or tool calls made in the wrong order. Users notice this as degraded product quality, not as an error page, and the symptoms may be inconsistent across sessions because of the model's non-determinism.

Without CI/CD integration, these regressions are caught late — in manual review, in user feedback, or in a post-incident review after the fact. The cost of late detection is high: lost user trust, rollback complexity, and the need for human triage that scales poorly as the team grows. Automated testing in CI/CD compresses the detection window to minutes after a commit, giving the author immediate feedback and preventing regressions from reaching production in the first place.
quality drops in accuracy, task completion rate, or safety metrics are caught at the pull-request stage, before code reaches main.
Deterministic fast feedback from unit tests:
mocking the LLM and tool calls makes unit tests run in seconds at zero API cost, giving the same fast feedback loop as conventional software.
running the same evaluation dataset on every commit produces a score timeline. Dips are immediately visible and attributable to a specific change.
the CI pipeline can block a staging or production deploy when the eval score drops below a threshold, removing manual approval as the only safety net.

agent execution traces captured during CI runs are stored as artifacts. When a test fails, the engineer can inspect the exact sequence of LLM calls and tool invocations that led to the failure, rather than trying to reproduce it locally.
because prompts and model configuration pass through the same CI pipeline as code, every change is tracked in version control and verified before deployment.

running a real LLM-based eval gate on every push is expensive. A 500-case eval dataset running a judge model adds material token cost per CI run. Teams typically run full evals on PRs targeting main but skip them on every feature branch push.

LLM-as-judge scores have sampling variance. A threshold that blocks deployment when accuracy drops by 2% can produce spurious failures if the judge itself fluctuates. Mitigation requires averaging over multiple samples or using temperature=0 on the judge model.
an eval dataset that was relevant six months ago may no longer cover the system's actual use cases. If the dataset is not maintained, the CI gate provides false assurance.

unit tests mock the LLM, so they cannot detect quality regressions caused by a prompt change. Eval gates cover quality but are too slow and expensive to run on every possible edge case. The coverage gap between the two tiers is real and must be accepted.

agent CI pipelines have more moving parts than traditional pipelines — fixture management, trace storage, score baselines, eval runners, and artifact publishing. This complexity adds maintenance overhead.
An agent CI/CD test suite is stratified into tiers by speed, cost, and coverage, following the same pyramid shape as traditional test suites but adapted for non-deterministic systems.
At the base of the pyramid are

. These replace every call to the LLM with a mock that returns a fixed, pre-authored response. Tool calls (web search, database queries, code execution) are replaced with stubs that return controlled outputs. The agent's orchestration logic — routing decisions, state machine transitions, tool selection — is exercised without touching any real external service. These tests run in a few seconds on a standard CI runner and cost nothing in API tokens.

. These run the full agent loop but replace expensive or flaky external services with stubs or lightweight simulators. The LLM API may be replaced with a recorded response fixture (VCR-style) or a very small local model. External HTTP services are intercepted with a library like

. A real database (Postgres, SQLite, or Redis running in a Docker service container) is used so that data persistence is real. These tests run in minutes and verify that the agent's components are wired together correctly.
At the top of the pyramid are

. These run the agent against a curated benchmark dataset using a real LLM (or a designated judge model) and measure output quality across multiple dimensions: task completion rate, accuracy relative to a reference answer, refusal rate on policy-safe inputs, toxicity score, and latency percentiles. The pipeline records the scores and compares them against a stored baseline. A drop below a threshold (e.g., accuracy must not fall more than 2% from the baseline) blocks the deployment. These tests are expensive — they make real API calls — so they run once per PR targeting a protected branch, not on every push.

is wired in from the start. Agent execution traces — the ordered sequence of LLM calls, retrieved context, tool invocations, and responses — are captured during test runs using a tracing library (LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, or the OpenTelemetry-compatible SDK for the framework in use). These traces are uploaded as CI artifacts and linked in PR comments alongside the eval scores. When a test fails or a score drops, the engineer can inspect the exact trace rather than trying to reproduce the failure from a stack trace.

attrs.alt: Agent CI/CD testing pipeline: Code Push → Build → Unit Tests (mocked LLM) → Integration Tests (stubbed APIs) → Eval Gate (LLM judge) → Staging Deploy → Production, with supporting panels for mock layer, eval harness, observability, and failure handling

content.text: The pipeline runs left to right. A code push (or pull request) triggers the CI workflow. The build stage installs dependencies, runs linting and type checks, and builds a Docker image that will be used in downstream stages. Unit tests run against the image immediately, using only mocked LLM responses and tool stubs — they complete in under a minute and are the first blocking gate. Integration tests follow, running the full agent loop with stubbed third-party HTTP calls and a real database service container. If integration tests pass, the eval gate runs the curated benchmark using a real (or judge) LLM and computes accuracy, completion, and safety scores. Staging deployment and production rollout are gated behind the eval gate result.

Supporting the pipeline are four cross-cutting concerns shown in the lower panels: the mock/fixture layer (controlling LLM responses in unit tests), the eval harness (measuring quality scores), the observability stack (capturing traces per run), and failure handling (blocking merges, storing artifacts, posting diff reports to PR comments).

Automated agent testing in CI/CD is appropriate whenever the agent will be deployed to production and updated regularly. The minimum threshold is an agent that receives user traffic, is iterated on by a team, and has any form of quality requirement — even informal ones. If those conditions are met, the cost of building a test pipeline is almost always lower than the cost of the first undetected regression.
Specific scenarios where CI/CD testing is especially important:
when the system prompt or few-shot examples change often, an eval gate detects quality changes automatically rather than relying on manual spot-checks.
Multiple engineers work on the agent:
CI prevents one engineer's change from silently regressing another's work without either noticing until after deployment.
when upgrading from one model version to another (e.g., claude-3-5-sonnet to claude-sonnet-4-5), the eval gate surfaces capability differences on the team's actual tasks before the upgrade ships.
when a tool's API changes, integration tests immediately reveal whether the agent still calls the tool correctly and handles the new response shape.

CI/CD testing is less immediately critical for pure research prototypes or single-user scripts that will be run once and discarded. It also adds overhead early in a project when the prompt and tool set change so frequently that maintaining a stable eval dataset is impossible — in that phase, manual review is more practical. Automate testing once the agent's core capabilities are stable enough that a benchmark dataset remains valid for at least a few weeks.
The following steps assume a Python agent using the Anthropic SDK, pytest, and GitHub Actions as the CI platform. The same principles apply to other stacks.
Step 1 — Separate pure logic from LLM calls
The most important architectural prerequisite is that the agent's orchestration logic is separable from the LLM API call. If the agent is a single monolithic function that calls
inline, it cannot be unit tested without hitting the real API. Refactor the LLM call behind an injected dependency — a function or object that the test can replace with a mock.
Step 2 — Write unit tests with mocked LLM responses
to intercept the HTTP call. Author fixture responses that cover the scenarios each test needs. Group fixtures in a
directory and load them with pytest fixtures so they are reusable across test modules.
Step 3 — Write integration tests with service containers
to spin up Postgres, Redis, or other dependencies as Docker containers alongside the test runner. Stub external HTTP services with
or a recorded VCR cassette. Run the full agent loop against these controlled dependencies and assert on side effects (records created in the database, messages posted to a queue).

Collect 50–200 representative input cases. For each case, record a reference answer or a rubric. Cases should cover the agent's most important capabilities, edge cases, and known past failures. Store the dataset as a JSON or CSV file in the repository so it is versioned alongside the code.
Step 5 — Write an eval runner and score baseline
Write a script that runs the agent on each eval case, computes scores (accuracy, completion rate, latency), and compares them against a

file stored in the repository. The script exits with a non-zero status code if any metric drops more than the allowed delta. Initialize the baseline by running the eval on the current version and committing the output.
Create a GitHub Actions workflow (or equivalent) with three jobs:
(runs on push to any branch, medium speed), and
(runs only on PRs targeting main, slow but gating). Set the eval gate job as a required status check for merging to main.
Upload agent execution traces as CI artifacts using
. Post a summary of eval scores as a PR comment using the GitHub API, including a diff from the baseline so reviewers can see which metrics changed and by how much.
Unit test with mocked Anthropic API

# tests/test_agent_unit.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from my_agent.agent import ResearchAgent


def make_mock_response(text: str):
    """Build a minimal Anthropic SDK response object for mocking."""
    message = MagicMock()
    message.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    message.content = [MagicMock(type="text", text=text)]
    return message


@pytest.fixture
def mock_anthropic():
    """Patch the Anthropic client so no real API calls are made."""
    with patch("my_agent.agent.anthropic.Anthropic") as MockClient:
        instance = MockClient.return_value
        yield instance


def test_agent_returns_answer_on_simple_query(mock_anthropic):
    mock_anthropic.messages.create.return_value = make_mock_response(
        "Paris is the capital of France."
    )
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_anthropic)
    result = agent.run("What is the capital of France?")

    assert "Paris" in result
    # Verify exactly one LLM call was made (no unnecessary retries)
    mock_anthropic.messages.create.assert_called_once()


def test_agent_uses_search_tool_when_needed(mock_anthropic):
    # First call: model asks to use the search tool
    tool_use_block = MagicMock()
    tool_use_block.type = "tool_use"
    tool_use_block.name = "web_search"
    tool_use_block.id = "tu_abc"
    tool_use_block.input = {"query": "latest AI research 2025"}

    first_response = MagicMock()
    first_response.stop_reason = "tool_use"
    first_response.content = [tool_use_block]

    # Second call: model synthesises the search result
    second_response = make_mock_response("Key 2025 AI findings: ...")

    mock_anthropic.messages.create.side_effect = [first_response, second_response]

    agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_anthropic)
    result = agent.run("What are the latest AI research findings?")

    assert mock_anthropic.messages.create.call_count == 2
    assert len(result) > 0

# scripts/run_eval_gate.py
"""Run the eval gate and exit non-zero if scores drop below baseline."""
import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import anthropic

EVAL_DATASET = Path("evals/dataset.json")
BASELINE_FILE = Path("evals/baseline.json")
ACCURACY_DROP_THRESHOLD = 0.03   # allow at most 3% drop
COMPLETION_DROP_THRESHOLD = 0.05


def judge_response(question: str, reference: str, response: str, client) -> float:
    """Use LLM-as-judge to score the agent response (0.0 – 1.0)."""
    prompt = (
        f"Question: {question}\n"
        f"Reference answer: {reference}\n"
        f"Agent response: {response}\n\n"
        "Score the agent response from 0 to 10 based on accuracy and completeness. "
        "Output only the integer score."
    )
    result = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",  # cheap judge model
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    try:
        return int(result.content[0].text.strip()) / 10.0
    except ValueError:
        return 0.0


def main():
    from my_agent.agent import ResearchAgent

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=client)
    dataset = json.loads(EVAL_DATASET.read_text())
    baseline = json.loads(BASELINE_FILE.read_text())

    scores = []
    completed = 0
    for case in dataset:
        try:
            response = agent.run(case["input"])
            score = judge_response(case["input"], case["reference"], response, client)
            scores.append(score)
            completed += 1
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"FAILED case {case['id']}: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
            scores.append(0.0)

    accuracy = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    completion_rate = completed / len(dataset)

    print(f"Accuracy:        {accuracy:.3f}  (baseline {baseline['accuracy']:.3f})")
    print(f"Completion rate: {completion_rate:.3f}  (baseline {baseline['completion_rate']:.3f})")

    failures = []
    if baseline["accuracy"] - accuracy > ACCURACY_DROP_THRESHOLD:
        failures.append(
            f"Accuracy dropped {baseline['accuracy'] - accuracy:.3f} (threshold {ACCURACY_DROP_THRESHOLD})"
        )
    if baseline["completion_rate"] - completion_rate > COMPLETION_DROP_THRESHOLD:
        failures.append(
            f"Completion dropped {baseline['completion_rate'] - completion_rate:.3f}"
        )

    if failures:
        print("\nEVAL GATE FAILED:", file=sys.stderr)
        for f in failures:
            print(f"  - {f}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    print("\nEval gate passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# .github/workflows/agent-ci.yml
name: Agent CI

on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  unit-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      - run: pytest tests/unit/ -v --tb=short

  integration-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: agent_test
        ports: ["5432:5432"]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      - run: pytest tests/integration/ -v --tb=short
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:test@localhost:5432/agent_test
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: integration-traces
          path: test-traces/
          retention-days: 30

  eval-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    needs: [unit-tests, integration-tests]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      - run: python scripts/run_eval_gate.py
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: eval-results
          path: eval-output/
          retention-days: 90

Consider a customer support agent deployed by a mid-sized SaaS company. The agent handles tier-1 support tickets: it reads the customer's description, searches a knowledge base via a tool, drafts a reply, and escalates to a human if the problem is outside scope. The team ships prompt improvements and new tool integrations several times per week.

The CI pipeline has three jobs. The unit-test job mocks the Anthropic API and the knowledge-base search tool and verifies that the orchestration logic correctly routes tickets by category, invokes the search tool when appropriate, and formats the draft reply. These 80 tests run in 25 seconds. The integration-test job runs the agent against a VCR-recorded set of API responses and a seeded SQLite knowledge base — it verifies that the full pipeline from ticket ingestion to draft reply works end to end, including database writes. This takes 90 seconds.

The eval gate job runs on PRs to main. It submits 120 historical tickets (drawn from a labelled sample of real customer support interactions with personally identifiable information removed) to the live agent. Each response is scored by

as judge on three dimensions: answer correctness, appropriate escalation decision, and professional tone. The scores are compared against a baseline committed when the current version was released. If correctness drops more than 3% or the escalation accuracy drops more than 5%, the PR is blocked. The eval gate costs approximately $0.40 per run at current Haiku pricing.

Traces from both the integration tests and the eval gate are uploaded as GitHub Actions artifacts and retained for 90 days. When an eval score drops on a PR, the team opens the trace artifact, inspects the cases that scored lowest, and can see exactly which tool calls were made, what the knowledge base returned, and what the model output. This reduces debug time from hours of manual reproduction to minutes of trace inspection.

# Example: integration test for the support agent
import pytest
import respx
import httpx
from my_agent.support_agent import SupportAgent
from my_agent.db import Database


@pytest.fixture
def seeded_db(tmp_path):
    db = Database(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/test.db")
    db.migrate()
    db.seed_knowledge_base([
        {"id": "kb-001", "title": "Password reset", "body": "Visit /reset-password..."},
        {"id": "kb-002", "title": "Billing FAQ", "body": "Invoices are sent monthly..."},
    ])
    return db


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_password_reset_ticket_resolved_without_escalation(seeded_db):
    # Record the expected LLM HTTP interaction using respx
    with respx.mock(base_url="https://api.anthropic.com") as mock:
        mock.post("/v1/messages").return_value = httpx.Response(
            200,
            json={
                "id": "msg_test",
                "type": "message",
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Please visit /reset-password to reset."}],
                "stop_reason": "end_turn",
                "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5",
                "usage": {"input_tokens": 100, "output_tokens": 20},
            },
        )

        agent = SupportAgent(db=seeded_db)
        result = await agent.handle_ticket(
            ticket_id="T-100",
            customer_message="How do I reset my password?",
        )

    assert result.draft_reply is not None
    assert "reset-password" in result.draft_reply
    assert result.escalated is False
    # Verify the ticket was written to the database
    ticket = seeded_db.get_ticket("T-100")
    assert ticket.status == "draft_sent"
Running real LLM calls in unit tests:

this makes unit tests slow (2–30 seconds per test), expensive ($), and flaky (network errors, model variability). Always mock the LLM in unit tests. If a test genuinely needs a real LLM call, classify it as an integration or eval test, not a unit test.
Setting the eval threshold too tight:

a threshold of 0.1% accuracy drop will cause spurious CI failures due to judge model sampling variance. Start with a 2–5% threshold and tighten it only after the pipeline has been running for a month and the variance is well understood.
Using a single temp=1 judge call per case:

a single LLM judge call at the default temperature introduces high variance. Either set temperature to 0 on the judge, or average three judge calls per case. The extra cost is small relative to the reduction in false positives.

if the baseline was computed on a version of the agent that was later significantly improved, the baseline is too low and the eval gate provides little protection. Update the baseline when intentional quality improvements are made.
Not uploading traces as CI artifacts:

without stored traces, a failing CI run provides only a score — not the information needed to diagnose why it failed. Always upload traces, even for passing runs (useful for later regression analysis).
Skipping the eval gate for hotfixes:
hotfix branches often bypass the eval gate to move fast. This is reasonable in a genuine incident but should be tracked. Add a required label (e.g.,
) that must be applied manually and notifies the team, so the bypass is intentional and visible rather than silent.
Make unit tests the first blocking gate:
unit tests should run before integration tests and eval gates, and they should block the pipeline on failure. Because they are fast and free, there is no reason to defer them.
Seed eval datasets from real traffic:

hand-authored eval cases miss real-world edge cases. Sample inputs from production traffic (with PII removed), label them, and add them to the dataset. This ensures the eval covers what users actually ask.

the LLM-as-judge does not need to be the most capable model. A smaller, cheaper model (claude-haiku or similar) is often sufficient as a judge and keeps eval gate costs low. Reserve the large model for the production agent itself.
Cache eval results on unchanged inputs:

if the prompt and model did not change but another part of the codebase did, the eval results from the previous run are still valid for the unchanged cases. Cache at the case level (keyed on input hash + prompt version) to avoid redundant API calls.
place fixture JSON files in a
). This makes it easy to audit what each test is simulating.
use the GitHub API to post the eval score diff as a formatted table in the PR comment. Reviewers see at a glance whether accuracy went up or down and by how much, without needing to open the CI logs.
or a thread pool to run eval cases concurrently (respecting the API rate limit). A 200-case eval that takes 10 minutes sequentially can finish in under 2 minutes with parallelism.

Pipeline runtime is the primary performance concern for agent CI. As the number of eval cases grows and the agent becomes more capable (and therefore slower per case), the eval gate job can balloon from 2 minutes to 20 minutes, creating friction for developers waiting for CI feedback.
The key levers for controlling eval gate runtime:

run eval cases concurrently against the rate limit. The Anthropic API defaults to 1,000 requests per minute (RPM) for Sonnet; a 200-case eval can be parallelized up to that limit. Use a semaphore or a rate-limiter wrapper to stay within bounds.
for fast feedback on feature branches, run only a 20% random sample of the eval dataset. Reserve the full dataset for PRs to main. This reduces eval cost by 5x on most pushes.

if the system prompt and model version are unchanged, cache eval scores at the case level. Only re-run cases affected by the change (e.g., cases that exercise the changed tool). This is complex to implement but cuts eval cost significantly for infrastructure-only changes.

run unit and integration tests on shared runners (cheap), but run the eval gate on a larger runner with more CPU/RAM so the Python eval orchestrator does not become the bottleneck when parallelizing many concurrent API calls.

At scale (eval datasets of 1,000+ cases), consider running the eval gate asynchronously as a separate workflow triggered by PR labelling rather than on every push to the PR branch. This decouples the eval from the fast unit-and-integration feedback loop.
Unit and integration tests; fixtures, parameterize, asyncio plugin
Mock httpx requests — works with Anthropic SDK which uses httpx under the hood
Supports route matching and response recording
Patch Anthropic client, tool functions, and database calls in unit tests
Capture traces, run eval datasets, compare score runs across commits
Open-source alternative to LangSmith; self-hosted trace store + eval runner
YAML-configured prompt evaluation; built-in CI integration; supports multiple providers
Workflow YAML, service containers, required status checks, artifact upload
Most common; similar to GitLab CI
Vendor-neutral trace export from agent code; import into LangSmith, Arize, or Jaeger
Fragile; only works for fixed-format outputs
High but slow; does not scale
High-traffic agents where offline eval dataset coverage is poor
CI pipelines for agents handle the production LLM API key and, in integration test jobs, may connect to production-adjacent databases. Security discipline is essential.

Store the Anthropic API key (and any other production credentials used in the eval gate) in the CI platform's encrypted secret store (GitHub Actions Secrets, GitLab CI variables, HashiCorp Vault). Never hard-code keys in workflow YAML or commit them to the repository. Use separate API keys for CI vs. production so that a compromised CI environment cannot affect the production quota or billing account. Set a spending cap or budget alert on the CI key.

When seeding the eval dataset from production traffic, remove all personally identifiable information (PII) before committing the dataset to the repository. Use a sanitisation script that strips names, email addresses, account numbers, and other identifiers. Store the raw labelled data in a private, access-controlled location separate from the code repository.

Eval datasets that include adversarial inputs (for testing the agent's safety guardrails) should be clearly labelled and isolated. Ensure that the eval runner does not inadvertently execute tool calls triggered by adversarial inputs against real production infrastructure. Run adversarial eval cases with all tool call execution disabled, or with tool stubs that log the attempted call and return a safe response.

The eval gate job is inherently less reliable than deterministic unit tests because it depends on the LLM API, which may have outages or rate-limiting events. Implement retry logic with exponential back-off in the eval runner. If the LLM API is unavailable and all retries are exhausted, fail the job with a clear error message (not a score-based failure) so the team knows the eval did not run, rather than interpreting an infrastructure failure as a quality regression.

Agent CI/CD testing is stratified: fast mocked unit tests catch logic bugs, integration tests verify wiring, and eval gates measure output quality — each tier serves a different purpose and has a different cost profile.
Always mock the LLM in unit tests using
; running real API calls in unit tests makes them slow, expensive, and flaky.

An eval gate compares quality metrics (accuracy, task completion, latency) against a committed baseline and blocks deployment when a metric drops beyond a threshold — it is the automated equivalent of a human QA review.
LLM-as-judge scoring has variance; use temperature=0 on the judge or average multiple judge calls per case to reduce spurious CI failures from sampling noise.

Execution traces captured during CI runs are the most valuable debugging artifact; upload them to artifact storage on every run so regressions can be diagnosed by inspecting the exact LLM calls and tool invocations that produced the failing output.
Eval datasets must be seeded from real production traffic (with PII removed) to remain representative; hand-authored datasets drift from actual usage over time.
Run eval cases in parallel against the API rate limit to keep eval gate wall-clock time under 5 minutes; sequential evaluation of a 200-case dataset is unnecessarily slow.

Store the Anthropic API key in CI secrets, use a dedicated CI key with a spending cap, and strip PII from eval datasets before committing — these are the minimum security requirements for a production agent CI pipeline.
